Source: MEDCORP
Query: Which of the following vitamin is synthesized in vivo, in the body by humans?

Options:
A: Niacin
B: Pantothenic acid
C: Cyanocobalamin
D: Folic acid
Retrieved Documents:
Document [0] (Title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Introduction) Vitamins play a vital role in many biochemical functions in the human body and are essential components for maintaining optimal health. There are two main groups of vitamins – fat-soluble (easily stored in fat upon absorption) and water-soluble (washed out and not easily stored). Although adequate intake of all vitamins is important, regular intake is required to avoid deficiency due to the transient nature of water-soluble vitamins. The water-soluble vitamins include Vitamin C and Vitamin B complex (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pantothenic acid, pyridoxine, biotin, folate, and cobalamin).
Document [1] (Title: Vitamin B3 -- Review Questions) Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.
Document [2] (Title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Clinical Significance) Vitamins are classified into two categories based on how they are absorbed and if they are stored. Water-soluble vitamins dissolve in water upon entering the body. Because of this, humans cannot store excess amounts of water-soluble vitamins for later use. There are nine water-soluble vitamins: the B vitamins -- folate, thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pantothenic acid, biotin, vitamin B6, and vitamin B12 -- and vitamin C. Deficiency of any of these water-soluble vitamins results in a clinical syndrome that may result in severe morbidity and mortality.

Document [17] (Title: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin) -- Indications) Cobalamin consists of four compounds with different biological functions, although these molecules are chemically similar. Cobalamine is a tetrapyrrolic corrin ring with central cobalt moiety. Cyanocobalamin and hydroxocobalamin are synthetic forms of cobalamin, whereas adenosylcobalamin (AdoCbl) and methylcobalamin have a biological activity to act as cofactors in enzymatic reactions that play a role in the synthesis of DNA, myelin, and fatty acids, which are vital for cell division and growth. The bioavailability of cyanocobalamin and hydroxocobalamin is different, has distinct pharmacologic properties, and can be used according to approved standards in a particular country. [1] [2] [3] [4] [5]

Document [22] (Title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Mechanism of Action) Vitamin B5 is exclusively a precursor in coenzyme A (CoA) synthesis and thus only directly affects this metabolic pathway. CoA has a role in hundreds of human biochemical reactions, such as cell growth, intermediary metabolism, and neurotransmitter synthesis. [14] [15] The structure of CoA functions as a carbonyl-activating group and as an acyl group carrier to help facilitate these various reactions. [16]
Document [23] (Title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Indications) Vitamin B5 (pantothenic acid) is a naturally occurring substance in various plants and animals (ie, eggs, milk, vegetables, beef, chicken, and whole grains). Vitamin B5 is also supplementally added to foods. Deficiency of this vitamin is rare but can present in people with severe malnutrition. A patient with vitamin B5 deficiency commonly has deficiencies in other nutrients, making it challenging to identify vitamin B5 deficiency specifically. An experimental vitamin B5 deficiency study associated the deficiency with symptoms such as fatigue, headache, malaise, personality changes, numbness, muscle cramps, paresthesia, muscle/abdominal cramps, nausea, and impaired muscle coordination. [1]

Document [2] (Title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1379) Niacin, also known as vitamin B3, is classified as a member of the vitamin B complex and is a common active ingredient in a wide variety of multivitamin and iron‑containing supplements. These products, which are available in capsule, tablet, liquid‑filled and powder forms, frequently combine niacin with other B‑vitamins such as thiamine (vitamin B1), riboflavin (vitamin B2), pyridoxine (vitamin B6), cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12), biotin, and calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5). In addition, the formulations often include vitamin C (ascorbic acid), vitamin D (cholecalciferol), folic acid, and a range of essential minerals including iron (ferrous fumarate or ferrous aspartate), zinc, copper, magnesium, manganese, and calcium. The niacin content varies widely among products, from low doses of about 1.8 mg to high‑potency levels of up to 800 mg, reflecting its use both for general nutritional support and for therapeutic purposes. Some supplement labels also note a relationship between pantothenic acid (kyselina pantothenová) and niacin, underscoring the interconnected role of B‑vitamins in metabolic health. Together, these combined formulations aim to address multiple micronutrient deficiencies, support energy metabolism, and promote overall patient well‑being.

Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 62) Vitamins constitute a broad therapeutic class that includes individual agents such as hydroxokobalamin (vitamin B12), cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin, folic acid (kyselina listová), vitamin A (plain and acetate), vitamin B1 (thiamine), vitamin B6 (pyridoxine), vitamin B12 (cobalamin family), vitamin C (ascorbic acid), vitamin D (cholecalciferol), vitamin E (α‑tocopherol acetate), and vitamin K₃, as well as combined forms (e.g., vitamin A + D, vitamin B‑complex). Multivitamin‑plus‑mineral preparations integrate these vitamins with essential minerals (calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide, copper oxide, magnesium‑L‑threonate, phosphorus, copper, zinc, calcium ascorbate, calcium threonate) and bioactive compounds (essential fatty acids, probiotics, NADH, flavin adenine dinucleotide, phosphatidylserine, betaine, succinic acid). Iron‑containing multivitamins are classified under “IRON/MULTIVITAMINS” and “iron in other combinations,” and they employ diverse iron sources—including
Document [29] (Title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1862) of anemia. By incorporating folic acid into multivitamin‑iron supplements, clinicians aim to correct or avert folate deficiency, support normal erythropoiesis, and improve overall hematologic health while simultaneously providing other essential vitamins (A, C, D, E, B‑complex) and minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, iodine) that contribute to broader metabolic and physiological functions.

Collectively, the community’s knowledge emphasizes that vitamin A, niacin (vitamin B3), calcium pantothenate (vitamin B5) and vitamin D are integral, often synergistic components of multinutrient products designed to correct nutritional deficiencies, support metabolic and antioxidant functions, promote bone health and, in the case of calcium pantothenate, contribute to wound healing, thereby addressing the needs of diverse patient populations.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1863) IRON/MULTIVITAMINS is a multivitamin formulation that incorporates iron and a broad spectrum of vitamins, minerals, and other nutrients to support overall health. The product is offered in several formats, including a Multi‑Day pack that adds calcium and extra iron, as well as oral capsule preparations marketed as Multivitamins w/ Iron. Its active ingredients are detailed in multiple ingredient listings: a vitamin B complex combined with vitamin C, folic acid, and iron (available as both tablets and capsules); a vitamin B complex plus vitamin C, folic acid, iron, and probiotics in capsule form; a vitamin B complex with folic acid, iron, and essential fatty acids in capsules; vitamin B12 paired with vitamin C, folic acid, and iron in capsules; vitamin B12 with folic acid and iron in capsules; and a liquid preparation that blends vitamins A, D, and C with iron. Additionally, a specific liquid ingredient, VITAMIN B/FERRIC PYROPHOSPHATE 100 MG/FA 0.25 MG, is included, providing a bioavailable source of iron. Classified as a “multivitamin agent,” IRON/MULTIVITAMINS serves as the active ingredient in these various formulations, delivering iron alongside nutrients that support hematopoiesis (folic acid, vitamin B12), antioxidant protection (vitamin C), bone health (calcium, vitamin D), and cellular function (essential fatty acids and probiotics). This integrated composition is intended to address iron deficiency while simultaneously supplying complementary vitamins and minerals essential for comprehensive patient care.
